chapter: Chapter 10: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

questions.question: Describe the structure and working of the human eye. Draw a labelled diagram.
questions.answer: The human eye is spherical. Light enters through cornea (refracts), passes pupil (iris controls size), lens (accommodates), forms inverted image on retina. Optic nerve carries signals to brain. Diagram: Cornea, iris, pupil, lens, ciliary muscles, retina, vitreous humour, optic nerve labelled.

questions.question: What is meant by power of accommodation? How does the eye focus on near and distant objects?
questions.answer: Power of accommodation is the ability of the eye to change focal length of lens. Ciliary muscles relax for distant objects (lens flattens), contract for near objects (lens thickens). Near point 25 cm, far point infinity in normal eye.

questions.question: Explain myopia with causes and correction method. Draw ray diagram.
questions.answer: Myopia: Near objects clear, distant blurred. Cause: Eyeball long or lens too curved; image before retina. Correction: Concave lens diverges rays to form image on retina. Diagram: Parallel rays converge before retina; concave lens corrects.

questions.question: Describe hypermetropia. Give its correction with ray diagram.
questions.answer: Hypermetropia: Distant clear, near blurred. Cause: Eyeball short or lens flat; image behind retina. Correction: Convex lens converges rays earlier on retina. Diagram: Rays from near object form behind retina; convex lens corrects.

questions.question: What is presbyopia? How is it corrected? What are bifocal lenses?
questions.answer: Presbyopia: Age-related difficulty in near vision due to weak ciliary muscles and less elastic lens. Correction: Convex part for near, concave for far in bifocal lenses. Allows seeing both distances without changing glasses.

questions.question: Explain dispersion of white light through a prism. Draw the diagram and label the spectrum.
questions.answer: White light splits into VIBGYOR when passing through prism due to different refraction angles for different wavelengths. Violet deviates most, red least. Diagram: Incident white beam, prism, emergent coloured spectrum labelled VIBGYOR.

questions.question: Describe how a rainbow is formed. Draw a ray diagram in a raindrop.
questions.answer: Rainbow forms by refraction, dispersion, internal reflection, and refraction again in raindrops. Primary rainbow: single internal reflection, red outside. Diagram: Ray enters drop, disperses, reflects internally, emerges as spectrum.

questions.question: Why does the sky appear blue to us? Explain the role of scattering.
questions.answer: Blue light scatters more because scattering ∝ 1/λ⁴ (shorter wavelength). Sunlight enters atmosphere, blue scatters in all directions, reaches eyes from sky. Red scatters less, passes straight.
answer_outline: Why blue seen from all directions

questions.question: Why do we see red colour of sun at sunrise and sunset?
questions.answer: Sunlight travels longer distance through atmosphere at horizon. Blue light scatters away, red light reaches observer directly. Hence sun appears red/orange.

questions.question: Explain why stars twinkle but planets do not.
questions.answer: Stars are point sources; atmospheric refraction causes random bending, intensity fluctuates (twinkling). Planets are disk sources; fluctuations average out, appear steady.

questions.question: What is atmospheric refraction? Give two examples from daily life.
questions.answer: Bending of light rays due to varying refractive index of atmosphere layers. Examples: Twinkling of stars, advanced sunrise and delayed sunset.

questions.question: Explain the Tyndall effect with two examples.
questions.answer: Scattering of light by colloidal particles making beam path visible. Examples: Headlight beam in fog, sunlight through tree canopy showing dust particles.

questions.question: Draw ray diagrams to show correction of myopia and hypermetropia.
questions.answer: Myopia: Parallel rays converge before retina; concave lens diverges to retina. Hypermetropia: Near rays converge behind retina; convex lens converges earlier. Diagrams with labels.

questions.question: Why does the sun appear flattened at sunrise and sunset?
questions.answer: Atmospheric refraction bends rays from lower part of sun more than upper part. Lower part appears raised, making sun look oval/flattened near horizon.

questions.question: Explain the role of rods and cones in the retina.
questions.answer: Rods: Sensitive to dim light, responsible for night vision (black & white). Cones: Sensitive to bright light, responsible for colour vision (three types: red, green, blue).

questions.question: Why are danger signals red in colour?
questions.answer: Red light has longest wavelength, scatters least, travels farthest in fog/smoke without loss. Hence visible from long distance, used for danger signals.

questions.question: Explain persistence of vision with an example.
questions.answer: Image formed on retina persists for ~1/16 second even after object removed. Example: Fan blades appear continuous circle when rotating fast; helps in movies (24 frames/sec appear continuous).

questions.question: Describe the experiment to show dispersion using a prism.
questions.answer: Pass narrow beam of white light through prism. Emergent light forms spectrum on screen (VIBGYOR). Violet deviates most, red least due to wavelength difference.

questions.question: Explain why we cannot see colours in dim light.
questions.answer: In dim light, cones (colour-sensitive) do not function well; only rods (low-light sensitive, no colour) work. Hence vision is black and white in low light.

questions.question: What is the cause of colour blindness? How does it affect vision?
questions.answer: Genetic defect; absence or defect in one or more types of cones. Most common: red-green colour blindness. Person cannot distinguish red and green shades.

questions.question: Explain how cataract affects vision and how it is treated.
questions.answer: Cataract: Lens becomes cloudy/opaque due to ageing or injury, reduces light entry, causes blurred vision. Treatment: Surgical removal of cloudy lens, replace with artificial intraocular lens.

questions.question: Why does the clear sky appear blue but clouds appear white?
questions.answer: Sky: Small molecules scatter blue more (Rayleigh). Clouds: Large water droplets scatter all wavelengths equally (Mie scattering), appear white.

questions.question: Explain the term 'far point' and 'near point' of the eye.
questions.answer: Far point: Farthest distance at which eye can see clearly without strain (infinity in normal eye). Near point: Closest distance for clear vision (25 cm in normal adult).

questions.question: Describe the role of cornea and crystalline lens in vision.
questions.answer: Cornea: Fixed convex surface, provides major refraction (~43 D power). Crystalline lens: Adjustable, provides accommodation (fine focusing) via ciliary muscles. Together focus light on retina.

questions.question: Give reasons for the following: (i) Sky blue (ii) Sun red at sunset (iii) Stars twinkle.
questions.answer: (i) Blue scatters more (1/λ⁴). (ii) Red travels longer path with less scattering. (iii) Atmospheric refraction causes random bending of starlight.
